result.title: Test Complete
result.subtitle: Your Baseline Cognitive State
result.description:
This is your current cognitive activity baseline. Retest regularly to see the trend—
trends matter more than a single score.
result.adviceDefault: Consistent practice will lead to improvement.

adviceTips.logic: Do one logic puzzle a day (e.g., Sudoku or reasoning puzzles) to gradually improve analytical skills.
adviceTips.math: Don't reach for a calculator right away when doing mental math—try calculating it yourself first, even if it's slow.
adviceTips.vocab: Read 10 minutes of a physical book or long-form article daily, paying attention to word choices and expressions.
adviceTips.event: Read long-form journalism or in-depth articles, paying attention to causal chains and logical connections between events.

result.retestPrompt: Retest in 7 days to track your changes
result.retestDesc: Cognitive ability is like a muscle—measure regularly to see the trend. Your history is saved locally.
result.flaggedCount: Flagged {count} possible issues. Thanks for the feedback!
result.improved: Your cognitive activity has improved since last time ↑
result.worsened: The degradation index has increased since last time, keep an eye on the trend
result.unchanged: Same as last time, stay attentive
result.author: Hsiang Nianian
result.logicGameDesc: Try playing <game>Cyber Deduction</game> to improve your focus and logical thinking through case-solving!
result.eventGameDesc: Try playing <game>Cyber Deduction</game> to train your event reasoning and analytical skills through case-solving!
result.logicEventGameTitle: Room for Logic & Event Reasoning Improvement
result.logicGoodDesc: Are you a programmer? Try <lcti>Another XingCe</lcti> and check your open-source karma!
result.disclaimer: Some questions are AI-generated in real-time and may contain errors. If you find any issues, feel free to join our community via the homepage. This is just a snapshot—one score doesn't define you. Don't take it too seriously.
result.scoringIrtDesc: This test used adaptive IRT estimation. Questions were selected based on your real-time ability level — each answer refined the estimate. Your final ability (θ) was mapped to a percentile via the normal distribution, then inverted to produce the degradation index. This means which questions you got right matters as much as how many.
result.scoringPctDesc: This test used fixed-percentage scoring. Your degradation index is simply the percentage of questions you got wrong: 100 − (correct / total × 100). Every question carries equal weight regardless of difficulty.
result.aiInterpretDesc: AI will generate a personalized cognitive analysis based on your test data
result.aiInterpretAnnounceDesc: You can now use AI analysis on the test results page to get a personalized cognitive analysis report based on your test data.

about.title: About
about.subtitle: Cognitive Rustproof · Project Origins
about.originP1: Since 2024, I've noticed myself increasingly throwing questions straight to AI instead of thinking them through first. My first reaction to a tough problem shifted from "let me think" to "let me ask." This shift is comfortable—but also unsettling.
about.originP2: "Cognitive Rustproof" isn't about denying the value of AI tools. It's about providing a simple way to regularly take a snapshot of your cognitive state. Not to prove anything, just to make the trend visible—because what you can't see, you won't care about.
about.usageP1: Complete {count} mixed-format questions (Logic / Mental Math / Vocabulary), each with a {seconds}-second time limit. After completion, you'll receive a cognitive activity score (0-100). The lower the score, the more active.
about.usageP2: A single test score has limited meaning—the real value lies in retesting. Leave at least 7 days between tests and connect the dots to see the trend.
about.privacyP1: We do not collect any personally identifiable information (no cookies, no user IDs). Each test result is stored anonymously and only used to generate platform-wide statistical distributions. Your personal history is saved in your browser locally and is not uploaded.
about.disclaimerP1: Some question types are AI-generated in real-time with adaptive difficulty. There may be errors or imprecise wording—please bear with us.
about.disclaimerP2: If you spot incorrect questions, feel free to join our community via the homepage to give feedback and help us improve the question bank.
about.disclaimerP3: This is a self-awareness tool, not a clinical assessment. A single score means little—don't take it too seriously. Trends matter more than any one result.
roadmapDoneItems: Adaptive assessment engine (IRT + EAP + Max Fisher + dimension rotation)
LLM question generation pipeline (DeepSeek + SSE streaming + Redis cache + rate limiting + logging)
Multi-language question bank (zh-CN 150 / en 148 / ja 146, covering IRT -3.0 ~ +3.0)
Global stats & personal trends (normal distribution/percentile/AI grouping/line chart/radar chart)
Sharing & retention (OG cards/challenge sharing/PWA/7-day reminder/theme toggle)
Multi-language UI (中文/English/日本語 + language switcher)
roadmapNearItems: AI question integration into test flow — Pure AI / Hybrid / Adaptive+AI modes
Question quality calibration — human review + statistical filtering for LLM-generated questions
Performance optimization — SSE timeout handling, cache hit rate monitoring, generation latency p50/p95 tracking
Test flow UX polish — adaptive mode UI, difficulty display, answer review
roadmapLongItems: Phase 2: Micro-Intervention Engine — dual Agent (assessment + coaching) delivers daily training based on your decline curve
Phase 3: Cognitive Mirror — four-Agent collaboration for cross-dimensional analysis, revealing blind spots the user doesn't see
Phase 4: Cognitive Rustproof Economy — employer matching, insurance pricing, decentralized cognitive credit ecosystem
about.irtP1: This test uses Item Response Theory (IRT), the same framework behind the SAT, GRE, and professional certification exams. Unlike simple percentage scoring, IRT accounts for which questions you got right, not just how many. Getting an easy question wrong hurts more; getting a hard question right boosts more.
about.irtP2: Each question has three parameters: difficulty (how hard it is), discrimination (how well it separates ability levels), and guessing (chance of guessing correctly). Together they form a 3PL logistic curve that models the probability of a correct answer at any ability level.
about.irtP3: During the test, after each answer the system re-estimates your ability (θ) and picks the next question to maximize Fisher information — the question that will tell us the most about you at your current estimated level. This is why the test can feel harder when you are doing well and easier when you are struggling.
about.irtP4: At the end, your ability θ is estimated via EAP (Expected A Posteriori), a Bayesian method that finds the most likely ability value given your full response pattern. The result is a theta score in logits, typically ranging from −3 to +3.
about.irtP5: Finally, your theta is mapped to a percentile using the standard normal distribution: θ = 0 (average) → 50th percentile → degradation index 50. θ = +1.5 (top ~7%) → degradation index ~7. This produces your final 0–100 degradation index where lower is better.

share.titleLine: See if AI has turned you into an idiot!

tier.cognitivePeakDesc: Your core cognitive abilities are in excellent shape. Even with frequent AI tool use, you maintain independent deep-thinking habits. You likely approach AI with critical thinking, treating it as a tool rather than a substitute.
tier.cognitivePeakAdvice: Keep up your current habits. You've proven that AI and independent thinking can coexist.
tier.mildDeclineDesc: Your cognitive abilities are generally good, but some early signs of decline are appearing. You may be starting to unconsciously rely on AI for certain types of thinking.
tier.mildDeclineAdvice: Before asking AI for help, give yourself 30 seconds to think independently. This small habit can effectively halt the decline.
tier.moderateDeclineDesc: Your cognitive activity has dropped noticeably. Daily over-reliance on AI for thinking tasks may be silently weakening your independent analytical skills.
tier.moderateDeclineAdvice: Set aside 15 minutes of AI-free deep reading/thinking time each day to let your brain get used to independent operation again.
tier.significantDeclineDesc: Significant cognitive decline detected. You may have grown accustomed to letting AI do the thinking that you should be doing yourself.
tier.significantDeclineAdvice: Take action now: 1) Reduce AI usage by 50% for one week 2) Do one logic puzzle daily without AI 3) Retest after one month.
tier.severeDeclineDesc: Your cognitive score is in the lowest range. Long-term reliance on AI to replace independent thinking has had a clear impact. Your brain is experiencing the "use it or lose it" effect.
tier.severeDeclineAdvice: Take this signal seriously. We recommend: 1) Start daily cognitive training immediately 2) Gradually extend AI-free thinking sessions 3) Develop a "think first, then ask AI" habit 4) Re-evaluate after one month.

list.intro: Thoughts on cognitive health, AI dependency, and reclaiming independent thinking.

ai-making-you-dumber.title: Is AI Making You Dumber?
ai-making-you-dumber.excerpt: A 5-minute cognitive snapshot that reveals what AI has left on your brain.
body: Since 2024, a quiet shift has happened. The first instinct when facing a hard problem is no longer 'let me think' — it's 'let me ask.' This shift is comfortable. It's also unsettling.
The 'Use It or Lose It' Problem

Cognitive ability works like a muscle. When you outsource thinking, the muscle atrophies. This isn't speculation — it's a well-established principle in cognitive neuroscience. The brain strengthens neural pathways it uses and prunes the ones it doesn't.
AI tools are incredible. They make us faster, more productive, and more capable. But when we use them to replace thinking rather than augment it, something is lost. The question is: how much?
Making the Invisible Visible
That's why we built Cognitive Rustproof. It's not an IQ test. It's a baseline measurement of your current cognitive state — a snapshot of how well your brain works when there's no AI to help.
20 questions. 40 seconds each. Logic, mental math, vocabulary. No AI assistance allowed. At the end, you get a 'degradation index' — a number between 0 and 100. Lower is better.
The Real Value Is the Trend
One score tells you little. Two scores, taken weeks apart, tell you everything. Is your index going up? That's a signal. Is it going down? Also a signal. Trends matter more than snapshots.
Think of it like stepping on a scale — except it measures cognitive activity, not body weight. The point isn't to judge. The point is to see.
Take the Test →

brain-fitness-guide.title: Brain Fitness: Reclaim Independent Thinking in 7 Days
brain-fitness-guide.excerpt: Seven daily habits to rebuild cognitive independence, whether you're showing early signs of AI dependency or just want to stay sharp.
body: Cognitive ability is like a muscle — use it or lose it. When AI does your thinking for you, neural pathways weaken. The good news: they can be rebuilt. Here's a 7-day program to reclaim independent thinking.
Day 1: The 'Think First' Rule

Before opening ChatGPT, Claude, or any AI tool, spend 60 seconds thinking independently. Write down your own answer first — even if it's incomplete. This simple habit creates a mental checkpoint that prevents automatic outsourcing of thinking.
Day 2: Read a Physical Book for 20 Minutes

No screens. No notifications. Just you and printed words. Physical reading activates different neural circuits than scrolling — it demands sustained attention, linear reasoning, and active imagination. Pick something slightly challenging.
Day 3: Do Mental Math

Calculate your grocery total before reaching the register. Split the dinner bill without your phone. Multiply two-digit numbers in your head while waiting for coffee. Mental math is one of the first cognitive skills to atrophy with calculator/AI dependency.
Day 4: Write 500 Words Without AI Assistance

No grammar checkers. No AI suggestions. No rewriting tools. Just you and a blank page. Write about something you care about. The goal isn't quality — it's the act of organizing thoughts into words without algorithmic assistance.
Day 5: Solve a Logic Puzzle

Sudoku. A chess problem. A riddle. A coding challenge on paper. Something that requires multi-step reasoning. Don't reach for hints. Sit with the discomfort of not knowing — this is where cognitive growth happens.
Day 6: Have a Deep Conversation

Talk to someone for an hour — no phones, no distractions. Argue a point. Explain something complex. Listen carefully and respond thoughtfully. Real-time conversation is the ultimate cognitive workout: you must think, process, and articulate simultaneously.
Day 7: Retest and Reflect
Take the Cognitive Rustproof test again. Compare your score to baseline. Did it change? What felt different this time? The number matters less than your awareness of it. Trends > snapshots.
Cognitive fitness isn't about rejecting AI — it's about maintaining the choice to think independently when it matters. Keep measuring. Keep noticing.
Check Your Baseline →

how-cognitive-test-works.title: How IRT-Based Cognitive Testing Actually Works
how-cognitive-test-works.excerpt: Behind the 20 questions is an Item Response Theory engine that adapts to your ability level. Here's how it produces a meaningful score.
body: Most online tests give you a fixed set of questions and a percentage score. Cognitive Rustproof does something different — it uses Item Response Theory (IRT), the same framework behind SAT, GRE, and professional certification exams.
What Is IRT?

Item Response Theory models the probability of a correct answer based on two things: the person's ability (theta) and the question's characteristics (difficulty, discrimination). Unlike percentage-correct scoring, IRT accounts for which questions you got right, not just how many.

Getting an easy question wrong hurts your score more than missing a hard one. Getting a hard question right boosts your score more than acing an easy one. This makes the score more informative than a simple percentage.
The Three Dimensions
The test covers three cognitive domains:

• Logic Reasoning — pattern recognition, logical deduction, sequential thinking
  • Mental Math — arithmetic fluency, numerical estimation, computational speed
  • Vocabulary — word knowledge, semantic understanding, verbal reasoning
Each dimension is calibrated independently, and the test rotates through them to build a balanced profile.
Adaptive Question Selection

As you answer questions, the system updates its estimate of your ability and selects the next question to maximize information gain. This is called EAP (Expected A Posteriori) estimation with Fisher information maximization.
In plain English: the test gets harder when you're doing well and easier when you're struggling — not to be cruel or kind, but because questions near your ability level provide the most information.
From Theta to Degradation Index

Your final theta estimate is mapped to a percentile using the normal distribution (standard normal CDF). The degradation index is simply 100 minus your percentile. A score of 50 means you're at the population average. A score of 10 means you're in the top 10%.
Why Trends Matter

No test is perfect. Measurement error exists. That's why we emphasize retesting — the trend across multiple measurements is far more reliable than any single score. Think of it like tracking your weight: one reading tells you little, but the trend over time tells you everything.
The goal isn't a number. It's awareness. Because what you can't measure, you won't improve.
Take the Test →
